walkthrough.title: Pour Out the Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's open the banana-shaped pouch and dump all 144 letter tiles face-down in the center of the table. This big pile of tiles is called the Bunch. Give them a good mix so nobody can track which letters are where.

walkthrough.title: Count Your Starting Tiles
walkthrough.text: With two to four players, each person takes 21 tiles. With five or six players, each person takes 15 tiles. With seven or eight players, each person takes 11 tiles.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Tiles
walkthrough.text: Keep them face-down in front of you. Do not flip them over yet. Nobody looks at their letters until the game begins.

walkthrough.title: Check the Bunch
walkthrough.text: The remaining tiles stay face-down in the center of the table. This is still the Bunch, and you will be drawing from it throughout the game. Make sure everyone can reach it easily.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Split
walkthrough.text: When everyone is set, any player calls out "Split!" and all players flip their tiles face-up simultaneously. The race begins the moment those tiles turn over.

summary.title: Pour Out the Tiles
bullets: Dump all 144 letter tiles face-down in the center
This center pool = the Bunch

summary.title: Count Your Starting Tiles
bullets: 2-4 players → 21 tiles each
5-6 players → 15 tiles each
7-8 players → 11 tiles each

summary.title: Draw Your Tiles
bullets: Each player grabs their tiles from the Bunch
Keep tiles face-down — no peeking

summary.title: Check the Bunch
bullets: Remaining tiles stay face-down in center
Everyone must be able to reach the Bunch

summary.title: Ready to Split
bullets: Any player calls "Split!" when everyone is ready
All players flip tiles face-up simultaneously
The race begins immediately

walkthrough.title: Build Your Own Crossword Grid
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what you are actually doing. The moment tiles flip face-up, everyone works simultaneously to arrange their letters into a personal crossword grid. Every word in your grid must connect to at least one other word, just like a crossword puzzle. There are no turns and no waiting.

walkthrough.title: Valid Words Only
walkthrough.text: Every sequence of two or more connected letters in your grid, reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, must be a real word. No proper nouns, no abbreviations, and no foreign words that are not in a standard English dictionary. If a group of letters is touching in a row or column, it must spell something valid.

walkthrough.title: Rearrange Freely
walkthrough.text: Let's clear up something that surprises new players. You can tear apart and rebuild your entire grid at any time. Nothing is locked in. If you realize a better layout exists, rip it all up and start over. There is zero penalty for rearranging.

walkthrough.title: Peel — Force Everyone to Draw
walkthrough.text: When you have used every single one of your tiles in your grid, you call out "Peel!" and every player, including you, must draw one tile from the Bunch. This is mandatory. You cannot hold back a Peel to be strategic. The moment all your tiles are placed, you must call it.

walkthrough.title: Dump — Trade Away Bad Letters
walkthrough.text: Let's say you are stuck with a Q and no U. You can Dump at any time by placing one unwanted tile face-down back into the Bunch and drawing three new tiles. That is a net gain of two tiles, so use it wisely. You do not need to announce a Dump to other players.

walkthrough.title: Bananas — How to Win
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the finish line. When the Bunch has fewer tiles remaining than the number of players and you use all your tiles, you call "Bananas!" instead of Peel. Other players then inspect your grid. If every word is valid, you win. If any word is invalid, you have a Rotten Banana and you are eliminated from the round.

walkthrough.title: Rotten Banana — Elimination
walkthrough.text: Let's be clear about what happens when you get caught. If you call Bananas and another player finds an invalid word in your grid, all of your tiles go back into the Bunch and you are out of the round. Play continues among the remaining players. This is why you should double-check every word before calling it.

walkthrough.title: No Score — Just Win or Lose
walkthrough.text: There is no point system in Bananagrams. The first player to call a valid Bananas wins the round, and that is it. You can play multiple rounds if you like, but each round is a clean, binary outcome.

bullets: All players work simultaneously — no turns

All words must interconnect

summary.title: Valid Words Only
bullets: Every 2+ letter sequence (left-right or top-bottom) must be a real word
No proper nouns, abbreviations, or foreign words

summary.title: Rearrange Freely
bullets: Tear apart and rebuild your entire grid anytime
Nothing is locked — zero penalty for rearranging

summary.title: Peel — Force Everyone to Draw
bullets: Use ALL your tiles → call "Peel!"
Every player draws 1 tile from the Bunch
Mandatory — can't hold back a Peel

summary.title: Dump — Trade Away Bad Letters
bullets: Return 1 tile to the Bunch → draw 3 back (net +2)
No announcement needed, use anytime
Good for clearing tough letters (Q, Z, X)

summary.title: Bananas — How to Win
bullets: Bunch has fewer tiles than # of players + you use all tiles → call "Bananas!"
Others inspect your grid — all valid words = you win
Any invalid word = Rotten Banana = eliminated

summary.title: Rotten Banana — Elimination
bullets: Invalid grid → all your tiles return to the Bunch
You are eliminated from the round
Play continues among remaining players

summary.title: No Score — Just Win or Lose
bullets: No point system — first valid Bananas wins
Binary outcome per round
Play multiple rounds if desired

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Bananagrams
walkthrough.text: Let's play through a four-player round together. We have Alex, Bailey, Casey, and Dana, each with 21 tiles face-down. Someone calls "Split!" and all four players flip their tiles over at once. The frantic word-building begins immediately.

walkthrough.title: Alex Starts Simple
walkthrough.text: Alex scans their letters and spots C-A-T right away. They lay down CAT, then see an R and an E. They extend it to CRATE, crossing the A with a short vertical word, AT. Simple, connected, and every tile used so far is part of a valid word. This is exactly the right approach for your opening move.

walkthrough.title: Bailey Goes Big
walkthrough.text: Bailey tries to show off by building MAGNIFICENT across the top of their grid. It uses eleven tiles in one word, but now the remaining ten tiles are nearly impossible to connect because the letters do not crossword easily with that long word. Bailey's grid grinds to a halt while everyone else keeps moving.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Start with Short, Flexible Words
walkthrough.text: Long words look impressive but they lock your tiles into rigid positions. Let's remember that short words with common letters give you more connection points and easier rearrangement. Build a web of three-letter and four-letter words first, then extend them as new tiles arrive.

walkthrough.title: Casey Gets Stuck on Q
walkthrough.text: Casey drew a Q but has no U among their tiles. They stare at the Q for over a minute, trying to think of a word. Meanwhile, the other three players are building, Peeling, and pulling further ahead. Casey refuses to give up the Q, convinced they will draw a U eventually.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Dump Difficult Letters Early
walkthrough.text: Let's not waste precious time hoping for the perfect partner letter. If you have a Q without a U, a Z with no vowel friends, or any letter that is stalling your progress, Dump it immediately. Yes, you draw three tiles back, but those three tiles will almost certainly be easier to use than the one tile paralyzing your grid.

walkthrough.title: Alex Calls Peel
walkthrough.text: Alex uses their last tile and calls "Peel!" All four players draw one tile from the Bunch. Alex already has a well-connected grid, so slotting in one new tile is easy. Bailey is still wrestling with MAGNIFICENT and now has one more tile to somehow attach. Casey finally Dumps the Q and starts rebuilding.

walkthrough.title: Dana Builds Steadily
walkthrough.text: Dana has been quietly building a tight grid of short, interlocking words. They call Peel three times in a row, forcing everyone to keep drawing. Their secret is flexibility. Every word in their grid shares letters with at least two other words, creating a dense crossword that absorbs new tiles easily.

walkthrough.title: The Bunch Gets Low
walkthrough.text: After several Peels, only two tiles remain in the Bunch. With four players, that is fewer tiles than players, so the next person to use all their tiles will call Bananas instead of Peel. The pressure is on. Alex and Dana are both close to finishing their grids.

walkthrough.title: Dana Rushes to Call Bananas
walkthrough.text: Dana places their last tile and shouts "Bananas!" The other three players stop and examine Dana's grid. Alex spots it right away. Dana has TIE running vertically, but reading horizontally through the same letters it spells QI, IX, and EL. Wait, one of those horizontal sequences is just XM, which is not a word. Dana has a Rotten Banana.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Every Direction Before Calling Bananas
walkthrough.text: Let's burn this into memory. When you place your last tile, do not call Bananas immediately. Scan every row left to right and every column top to bottom. Any sequence of two or more adjacent letters must be a valid word. One accidental two-letter combination you overlooked means elimination. Take five extra seconds to verify rather than losing the entire round.

walkthrough.title: Alex Wins the Round
walkthrough.text: Dana's tiles go back to the Bunch. Alex uses the extra time to finish their grid, triple-checks every row and column, and calmly calls "Bananas!" Bailey and Casey inspect it. Every word checks out. Alex wins with a clean grid built on short, flexible words and careful verification.

walkthrough.title: What We Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the three big lessons from this round. First, start with short flexible words instead of long impressive ones. Second, Dump difficult letters immediately instead of waiting for a miracle tile. Third, always verify your entire grid before calling Bananas because one invalid sequence means elimination.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text: Before you start your first real game, check out General Tips for more strategies on grid-building and tile management. And remember, you can always ask your personal GM Guide for help during the game. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question. Have fun going bananas.

Covers common mistakes and key lessons

Rotten Banana — Dana is eliminated

Always verify every row and column before calling Bananas

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → grid-building and tile management
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Build Short Words First
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the most important habit in Bananagrams. When you flip your tiles, resist the urge to build long words. Instead, build a core of three-letter and four-letter words that interlock like a crossword. Short words create more crossing points, which means more places to attach new tiles as they arrive from Peels.

walkthrough.title: Keep Your Grid Loose and Rearrangeable
walkthrough.text: New players build their grid and treat it as permanent. Do not do this. Your grid is a living thing that should change shape constantly. If a new tile does not fit, do not force it into a corner. Instead, look at your grid and ask what you can tear apart to make room. The best players rebuild portions of their grid multiple times per round.

walkthrough.title: Dump Early, Dump Often
walkthrough.text: The Dump costs you a net gain of two tiles, but it removes a problem letter and gives you three chances at something better. If you spend more than fifteen seconds staring at a tile, Dump it. The time you waste trying to use a difficult letter is worth far more than two extra tiles.

walkthrough.title: Memorize Key Two-Letter Words
walkthrough.text: Two-letter words are your secret weapon. Words like QI, ZA, XI, XU, JO, and KA let you use difficult letters without building long words around them. Let's commit as many two-letter words to memory as possible before playing. They turn problem tiles into quick connections.

walkthrough.title: Peel Pressure Is Real
walkthrough.text: Every Peel forces all players to draw, including opponents who might be struggling. Let's use this to our advantage. If your grid is flexible and absorbs tiles easily, you want to Peel as fast as possible. Rapid Peeling puts constant pressure on players with rigid grids while barely slowing you down.

walkthrough.title: Vowel and Consonant Balance
walkthrough.text: Let's keep a rough mental count of our vowels and consonants. Too many consonants with no vowels means you cannot form words. Too many vowels and you are stuck building awkward words. If your ratio gets heavily skewed, Dump a tile from the overloaded category to rebalance.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Watch out for these three traps. First, building one massive word that consumes half your tiles but offers only two crossing points. Second, leaving a tile sitting off to the side "for later" when you could Dump it and move on. Third, calling Bananas without scanning every row and column. Each of these mistakes can cost you the round.

walkthrough.title: Speed Versus Accuracy
walkthrough.text: Let's be clear about the tradeoff. Speed gets you to Bananas first, but accuracy keeps you from being eliminated. There is no prize for calling Bananas fast if your grid has an invalid word. Always take a few seconds at the end to scan your entire grid, reading every horizontal and vertical sequence. A calm five-second check is the difference between winning and a Rotten Banana.

summary.title: Build Short Words First
bullets: 3-4 letter words = more crossing points
More crossings = easier to attach new tiles
Resist the urge to build long words early

summary.title: Keep Your Grid Loose and Rearrangeable
bullets: Grid is not permanent — rebuild portions freely
If a tile doesn't fit, rearrange rather than force it
Best players rebuild multiple times per round

bullets: Net +2 tiles, but removes a problem letter
Staring at a tile for 15+ sec? Dump it
Time wasted > cost of 2 extra tiles

summary.title: Memorize Key Two-Letter Words
bullets: QI, ZA, XI, XU, JO, KA — use tough letters fast
2-letter words turn problems into quick connections
Study the full 2-letter word list before playing

summary.title: Peel Pressure Is Real
bullets: Every Peel forces ALL players to draw
Flexible grid + rapid Peeling = pressure on opponents
If your grid absorbs tiles easily, Peel fast

summary.title: Vowel and Consonant Balance
bullets: Track rough vowel/consonant ratio
Too many of one type → Dump from the overloaded category
Balanced tiles = easier word-building

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: One massive word with only 2 crossing points — too rigid
Leaving tiles aside "for later" — Dump them instead
Calling Bananas without scanning every row/column

summary.title: Speed Versus Accuracy
bullets: Speed gets you to Bananas — accuracy keeps you alive
Always scan every horizontal + vertical sequence before calling
5-second check = difference between winning and elimination

walkthrough.title: The Suffix and Prefix Engine
walkthrough.text: Let's level up your word-building with a simple technique. Keep common suffixes like -ING, -ED, -ER, and -TION in mind as you scan your tiles. When you spot these letter combinations sitting in your grid, build words that end with them. The same applies to prefixes like UN-, RE-, and PRE-. These patterns let you construct words almost automatically instead of searching letter by letter.

walkthrough.title: Grid Architecture — The Hub System
walkthrough.text: Let's think about grid design intentionally. Build your grid around hub words: short words with common letters that sit at the center of your crossword. A word like RATE with R, A, T, and E can branch in four directions. Place hub words centrally and attach satellite words to their letters. When you need to rearrange, the hubs stay and only satellites change.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Dumping
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about advanced Dump timing. Do not just Dump bad letters. Dump strategically when you have a nearly complete grid and one tile that does not fit. The three tiles you draw back have a high chance of including at least one common letter that slots right in. Also, Dump right after an opponent Peels to maximize the time you have before the next forced draw.

walkthrough.title: Peel Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: Let's weaponize the Peel. If you are a fast builder, Peel as rapidly as possible. Each Peel gives everyone one tile, but you can absorb tiles faster than your opponents. Over five or six rapid Peels, slower players accumulate a backlog of unplaced tiles while your grid stays clean. This tempo advantage is how top players dominate.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Scan
walkthrough.text: Let's master the most critical moment in the game: the seconds before you call Bananas. Develop a systematic scan pattern. Start at the top-left of your grid and read every horizontal sequence left to right, row by row. Then start at the top-left again and read every vertical sequence top to bottom, column by column. This takes five to ten seconds and catches accidental two-letter sequences that would cost you the game.

walkthrough.title: Letter Frequency Awareness
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the tile distribution. Bananagrams has more common letters like E, A, I, and N, and very few J, Q, X, and Z tiles. Knowing this changes your strategy. When you see rare letters, plan for them immediately rather than hoping to build around them later. When you see common letters, know that opponents likely have similar tiles and will be building similar words.

walkthrough.title: Parallel Word Building
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the most tile-efficient grid technique. Instead of only crossing words at single letters, place words parallel to each other so that every adjacent pair of letters forms a valid two-letter word. For example, placing TO next to AN creates both TA and ON as cross-words. This technique packs more tiles into less space and creates a dense grid that is easy to extend.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Let's use awareness of other players to our advantage. Watch for signs. If opponents are staring at their tiles without moving, they are stuck. This means Peeling right now puts maximum pressure on them. If opponents are rapidly placing tiles, a Dump might be smarter than a Peel since adding tiles to fast builders helps them. Read the table and adjust your tempo accordingly.

summary.title: The Suffix and Prefix Engine
bullets: Spot -ING, -ED, -ER, -TION in your tiles → build around them
UN-, RE-, PRE- prefixes enable quick word construction
Pattern recognition > letter-by-letter searching

summary.title: Grid Architecture — The Hub System
bullets: Hub words: short words with common letters (RATE, LINE, SPOT)
Place hubs centrally → attach satellite words to their letters
When rearranging, hubs stay, satellites change

summary.title: Strategic Dumping
bullets: Dump when 1 tile blocks a near-complete grid
3 new tiles likely include a common letter that fits
Time Dumps right after opponent Peels for max breathing room

summary.title: Peel Tempo Control
bullets: Fast builders → Peel rapidly to pressure slow opponents
Each Peel adds 1 tile to everyone — you absorb faster
5-6 rapid Peels create a tile backlog for struggling players

summary.title: The Endgame Scan
bullets: Systematic scan: all rows L→R, then all columns T→B
Takes 5-10 sec — catches accidental invalid sequences
Develop this as muscle memory before every Bananas call

summary.title: Letter Frequency Awareness
bullets: Common: E, A, I, N — expect multiples, plan flexible words
Rare: J, Q, X, Z — plan for these immediately when drawn
Know the distribution to anticipate what's in the Bunch

summary.title: Parallel Word Building
bullets: Place words parallel → adjacent letters form valid 2-letter words
Example: TO next to AN creates cross-words TA and ON
Packs more tiles into less space — dense, extendable grid

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Opponents staring and stuck → Peel now for max pressure
Opponents placing rapidly → Dump may be better than Peel
Read the table and adjust your tempo accordingly

entries.definition: The call that starts the game. Any player says "Split!" and all players simultaneously flip their starting tiles face-up. Play begins immediately — there is no countdown or delay.

Called when a player has used ALL their tiles in their grid. Every player (including the caller) must draw 1 tile from the Bunch. Mandatory — you must Peel the moment all tiles are placed. Cannot be called if the Bunch has fewer tiles than the number of players.

Return 1 unwanted tile face-down to the Bunch and draw 3 new tiles. Net gain of 2 tiles. Can be done at any time without announcing it to other players. No limit on how many times you can Dump per round.

Called instead of Peel when the Bunch has fewer tiles than the number of players and you have used all your tiles. This is the winning call. Other players stop and inspect your grid. If all words are valid, you win the round.

Each player's arrangement of letter tiles. All words must interconnect like a crossword puzzle — every tile must be part of the connected grid. You cannot have isolated words or tiles sitting apart from the main grid.

Every horizontal (left-to-right) and vertical (top-to-bottom) sequence of 2 or more adjacent tiles must form a valid English word. No proper nouns, abbreviations, or non-English words allowed. Standard dictionary is the authority.
Players may tear apart and rebuild any portion of their grid at any time during the game. There is no penalty or cost for rearranging. Nothing in the grid is ever locked in place.

A player wins by calling "Bananas!" when the Bunch is nearly empty (fewer tiles than players) and having every word in their grid verified as valid by the other players. First valid Bananas call wins the round.

When a player calls "Bananas!" but their grid contains one or more invalid words. That player is eliminated from the round and all their tiles are returned face-down to the Bunch. Play continues among remaining players.

A player who receives a Rotten Banana is out for the rest of the round. Their tiles go back into the Bunch, potentially extending the game for remaining players. There is no way to re-enter a round once eliminated.

The communal pool of face-down letter tiles in the center of the table. Players draw from the Bunch during Peels and Dumps. The Bunch shrinks as the game progresses. When it has fewer tiles than the number of players, the endgame begins.
The tiles each player draws face-down before the game begins. 21 tiles for 2-4 players, 15 tiles for 5-6 players, 11 tiles for 7-8 players. These are drawn from the Bunch during setup.

144 lowercase letter tiles, each showing a single letter. Distribution follows English letter frequency — more common letters (E, A, I) have more tiles. No blank or wild tiles in standard Bananagrams.

The game enters the endgame when the Bunch has fewer tiles remaining than the number of players. At this point, Peel is no longer possible. The next player to use all their tiles calls "Bananas!" to attempt a win.
